title: How firefighter parts create chaos to avoid feeling
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The firefighter who shows up too fast

You're in a conversation. Someone says something that brushes against an old wound. Nothing dramatic. Maybe they question your judgment. Maybe they don't respond to a text fast enough. And suddenly, you're not in the conversation anymore. You're in action mode.

Your body shifts. Your thoughts race. You start fixing, explaining, defending, solving, organizing, cleaning, checking email, making lists, changing the subject, cracking a joke, leaving the room. Anything but staying with what just happened inside you.

Here's the thing. That frantic energy isn't random. It's a part of you. In Internal Family Systems (IFS) language, Richard Schwartz calls it a firefighter part. And it has one job: put out the emotional fire by any means necessary.

Firefighter parts don't care about long-term consequences. They don't care about relationships. They don't care about your health. They care about one thing only. Making the feeling stop. Right now.

And they will burn down the whole house to do it.

What firefighters are actually protecting

Here's what most people miss about these parts. They look destructive. They look like the problem. But they're not the problem. They're a solution that's gone rogue.

Janina Fisher, who writes extensively about trauma and dissociation, describes these parts as protectors that learned their job in childhood. A child who couldn't escape an abusive parent couldn't fight back. Couldn't run away. Couldn't say no. So the child's system found other ways to survive. Distraction. Numbing. Perfectionism. People-pleasing. Rage. Shutting down.

Those strategies kept you alive then. They kept you from feeling things that would have overwhelmed a nervous system that wasn't ready to process them. Your firefighters did their job. They saved you.

But here's the hard truth. The danger is over. The abusive parent is gone. The unsafe environment is in the past. And your firefighters are still showing up, still fighting fires that burned out years ago, still creating chaos in your present life to avoid feelings that no longer threaten your survival.

Stay with me here. This isn't about blaming your parts. It's about understanding them. Because when you understand what a firefighter is actually protecting, you can stop fighting it. And start working with it.

The firefighter isn't trying to hurt you. It's trying to protect you from something it believes would destroy you. That something is almost always a feeling. Shame. Grief. Terror. Abandonment. Worthlessness. The feelings that were too much for a child to hold.

The chaos they create to keep you safe

Let me tell you what this looks like in real life. Because it's not always dramatic. Sometimes it looks like productivity. Sometimes it looks like caretaking. Sometimes it looks like being the most responsible person in the room.

I had a firefighter that worked through overthinking. I'd get a whiff of something uncomfortable, and suddenly I'd be analyzing the situation from every angle, predicting every outcome, preparing for every disaster. I looked like I was being careful. Thoughtful. Diligent. But really, I was just keeping myself from feeling. My mind was so busy constructing scenarios that there was no room for the actual sensation in my chest.

Another firefighter worked through busyness. Fill every hour. Never stop moving. Never sit still. Because stillness is where the feelings live. And that's terrifying when you've never learned that feelings can be felt without destroying you.

Some firefighters are louder. They create arguments. They pick fights. They find something to be angry about so they don't have to feel the sadness underneath. Anger is powerful. Anger is energizing. Anger keeps the vulnerable parts hidden behind a wall of righteous fury.

Some firefighters are quieter. They disappear into work. Into relationships. Into substances. Into sleep. Into scrolling. Into anything that provides a temporary escape from the internal pressure that builds when a feeling starts to surface.

Here's what all these firefighters have in common. They create chaos. External chaos, internal chaos, relational chaos. Because chaos is a distraction. And distraction is a form of protection.

Think about it. When you're in the middle of a crisis, when everything is on fire, when you're scrambling to put out flames, you don't have to feel the grief that's been waiting in the wings for years. You don't have to acknowledge the loneliness. You don't have to sit with the shame. You're too busy surviving.

And that's exactly what your firefighter wants.

What happens when you take the chaos away

This is where recovery gets tricky. Because your system doesn't know that the firefighter is creating the problem. Your system thinks the firefighter is saving you from something worse. So when you start trying to change the firefighter's behavior, when you start trying to sit still, when you start trying to feel instead of fix, your system panics.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how the nervous system needs to pendulate between activation and settling. It needs to approach the feeling, then back away, approach, then back away. It can't stay in the fire. But it also can't avoid it forever.

When you take away the firefighter's strategies, you're not just changing a habit. You're threatening the system's entire survival strategy. The firefighter will fight back. It will scream. It will create bigger fires. It will tell you that you're going to die if you don't do something. It will convince you that the feeling is too big, too dangerous, too overwhelming.

And here's the thing. It's not wrong. Not entirely. The feeling might be overwhelming. But not because the feeling itself is dangerous. Because your system has never learned that feelings can be felt without being destroyed by them.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work explains exactly how these firefighter parts operate and how to work with them. He calls them the fight and flight responses, and he maps out how they show up in everyday life. It's validating to see your own patterns described so clearly.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). This one helps you understand where your firefighters learned their strategies. Because they didn't come from nowhere. They came from a childhood where feelings weren't safe, where your parents couldn't hold your emotions, where you had to handle everything alone.

How to be with the firefighter without becoming it

So what do you do? You don't fire the firefighter. You don't tell it to shut up. You don't try to bypass it with positive affirmations or gratitude lists. That's toxic positivity dressed up as healing, and it just makes the firefighter work harder.

Instead, you get curious. You notice the firefighter without becoming it. You step back and observe the part that's revving up, that's creating urgency, that's telling you to do something, anything, to make the feeling go away.

You say something like, "I notice there's a part of me that really wants to fix this right now. That wants to make a plan. That wants to call someone and talk it through. That wants to clean the kitchen. I see that part. I know it's trying to protect me. And I'm going to try something different. I'm going to stay here for a minute and see what happens."

And then you feel it. The thing you've been avoiding. The shame. The grief. The loneliness. The terror. You feel it in your body. The tight chest. The burning throat. The hollow stomach. The shaking legs.

You don't try to make it go away. You don't analyze it. You don't tell a story about it. You just feel it. For thirty seconds. For a minute. For however long you can tolerate.

And then you back off. You take a breath. You look around the room. You notice something outside. You let your system settle. And then maybe you approach it again.

This is the pendulation Levine talks about. Approach and retreat. Feeling and settling. Over and over. Until your system learns that the feeling doesn't kill you. That it rises and falls like a wave. That you can survive it.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). Jonice Webb's work explains how emotional neglect creates these protectors in the first place. When your feelings weren't mirrored or validated as a child, your system learned that feelings were dangerous. And your firefighters stepped in to keep you safe from them.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work gives you the big picture of how trauma shapes the whole system. It helps you understand why your firefighters are so committed to their job. They're not stubborn. They're loyal. They've been doing this work for years, and they need to know you can handle what they've been protecting you from.

The slow work of earning their trust

Your firefighters won't retire overnight. They've been on duty for decades. They've seen you survive things that should have broken you. They don't trust that you can feel your feelings without falling apart. And honestly, they might be right. Not because you can't handle your feelings. But because you've never practiced.

So you practice. In small doses. You feel a little bit of the grief. You notice the firefighter that wants to distract you. You thank it. You feel a little more. You back off. You let your system integrate.

Wild, right? The thing that looks like the problem is actually the solution that's been working too hard. The chaos isn't the enemy. It's a signal. A cry for help from a part of you that's exhausted from carrying a burden it was never meant to carry.

Your firefighter wants to rest. It wants to stop putting out fires. But it can't until it knows that the feelings it's been protecting you from are safe to feel. And that's not something you can tell it. It's something you have to show it. One moment of staying present at a time.

Sit with that. Notice what just happened in your body. Did something tighten? Did something relax? Did a thought come in to pull you away from this moment?

That's your firefighter. Doing its job. Trying to protect you. And maybe, for the first time, you can see it not as an enemy, but as a tired guardian that needs to know you're okay.

You are. You're learning. And that's enough for now.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Running on Empty (paid link) - Jonice Webb's work on emotional neglect and its impact on the developing nervous system
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - Judith Herman's foundational text on understanding trauma's effects
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - Understanding how childhood patterns shape protective parts
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide to C-PTSD recovery and working with parts
